flags.message: The snapshot is very sparse for this SCOTUS original petition: it has no docket entries, no filing or disposition dates, no event description, and no visible cluster contents. The prediction was completed from the available snapshot, but confidence is materially limited.

The provisioned snapshot for this petition-disposition event is very sparse: it has no docket entries, filing or disposition dates, lower-court information, party details beyond the caption, or petition-stage materials. I produced a conservative prediction from the available snapshot and local aggregate priors.

unclassified.reason: docket Term not parseable (a format the predicate skips)

gaps.label: A base-rate prior surfaced from the corpus for SCOTUS original-jurisdiction / extraordinary-writ dispositions (distinct from the cert docket) would beat reasoning from memory and reduce cross-run variance.
A compact snapshot summary of linked cluster contents would materially improve sparse historical SCOTUS petition predictions.
A petition-specific prior that distinguishes cert-stage grants from merits case disposition labels would make sparse SCOTUS snapshots easier to calibrate.
A snapshot-derived flag distinguishing original-docket matters from certiorari petitions would make the strongest available signal explicit rather than inferred from the docket-number string.
Corpus-local examples keyed by event kind and disposition label with short explanations of label assignment
Richer petition-stage docket snapshots for historic Supreme Court cases
query date_decided/date_filed are null for these historical rows, so I could not weight priors by era

the snapshot carries an opinion-cluster URL but no way to read the cluster's disposition as legal context without it counting as a new case fact; a snapshot field that already classifies the linked cluster's disposition would disambiguate other-vs-dismissed

helpful.label: Corpus disposition counts provided useful base-rate calibration.
Existing local prediction artifacts clarified how opinion-cluster references are treated for SCOTUS petition events.
Prior claude-baseline petition-disposition predictions as a calibration anchor.
The materialized snapshot kept target-case facts point-in-time.
fedcourts paths resolved the event path.
fedcourts paths to resolve the snapshot/event paths.
fedcourts provision-snapshot provided the exact point-in-time snapshot path.

gaps: A compact snapshot summary of linked cluster contents would materially improve sparse historical SCOTUS petition predictions.
helpful: fedcourts paths resolved the event path.
The materialized snapshot kept target-case facts point-in-time.
Corpus disposition counts provided useful base-rate calibration.
recent.notes: I used fedcourts query and local corpus aggregate counts only for priors, not for additional target-case facts beyond the materialized snapshot.

gaps: A base-rate prior surfaced from the corpus for SCOTUS original-jurisdiction / extraordinary-writ dispositions (distinct from the cert docket) would beat reasoning from memory and reduce cross-run variance.
A snapshot-derived flag distinguishing original-docket matters from certiorari petitions would make the strongest available signal explicit rather than inferred from the docket-number string.
helpful: fedcourts paths to resolve the snapshot/event paths.
schemas/prediction.schema.json as the exact output contract.
Prior claude-baseline petition-disposition predictions as a calibration anchor.
recent.notes: Snapshot encodes no realized disposition (all disposition dates null, no docket entries) and no merits facts, so the call rests on the original-docket / ex parte structural signal and the extraordinary-writ base rate. Did not open the linked opinion cluster (that is the outcome under evaluation). Did not use the CourtListener MCP, to avoid pulling case-specific facts.

gaps: Richer petition-stage docket snapshots for historic Supreme Court cases
Corpus-local examples keyed by event kind and disposition label with short explanations of label assignment
helpful: fedcourts query for resolved SCOTUS priors
recent.notes: The snapshot was enough to make a conservative non-grant prediction, but it lacked docket entries and petition-stage dates. I used local corpus priors only for aggregate context and did not fetch new case facts.

gaps: the snapshot carries an opinion-cluster URL but no way to read the cluster's disposition as legal context without it counting as a new case fact; a snapshot field that already classifies the linked cluster's disposition would disambiguate other-vs-dismissed
query date_decided/date_filed are null for these historical rows, so I could not weight priors by era
helpful: fedcourts query --court scotus gave the realized disposition base rates (other 75% / dismissed 19% / denied 4.5% / granted 1.6%) that anchored the call
filtering query summaries for railroad/commission/mootness priors surfaced comparable historical dispositions
recent.notes: Thin snapshot (empty docket_entries, all dates null) for a pre-1925 mandatory-jurisdiction SCOTUS matter. The published opinion cluster signals a merits/dismissal disposition rather than a discretionary denial; base rates plus the railroad-regulation posture drove a dismissed call with granted=0.

gaps: A petition-specific prior that distinguishes cert-stage grants from merits case disposition labels would make sparse SCOTUS snapshots easier to calibrate.
helpful: fedcourts provision-snapshot provided the exact point-in-time snapshot path.
Existing local prediction artifacts clarified how opinion-cluster references are treated for SCOTUS petition events.
recent.notes: The corpus query output was broad, so the prediction relied primarily on the snapshot's cluster signal and local artifact conventions.
